content_items.title: Researchers Track Rapid Glacier Retreat to Model Climate Change Risks
content_items.summary: A campus-led team uses satellite imagery and on-site measurements to track glacier retreat, improving climate change risk forecasts for downstream communities.
content_items.body:
A multidisciplinary team of climate scientists, hydrologists, and data modelers from the College of Arts & Sciences spent three seasons documenting rapid glacier retreat in the Andes. Combining drone footage, satellite imagery, and historical records, the team developed new models that estimate how melting glaciers will affect downstream water supplies and flood risks over the next 30 years.

Lead researcher Prof. Elena Marquez explains that the project provides one of the most detailed looks yet at the pace of climate change in high-altitude environments. The work also incorporates input from local communities to understand social and economic impacts. The findings, published in the journal Climate Dynamics, will inform regional adaptation planning.

content_items.title: Studying Urban Heat Islands to Boost Climate Resilience
content_items.summary: Researchers map heat patterns in city neighborhoods to understand how climate change is amplifying extreme heat and to test cooling strategies.
content_items.body:
Faculty and students from the departments of Geography and Public Health partnered with local organizations to map street-level temperatures in historically underserved neighborhoods. Using wearable sensors and mobile weather stations, the team documented differences of up to 15 degrees Fahrenheit between shaded streets and highly paved areas.

The study highlights how climate change exacerbates existing inequities as extreme heat events become more frequent. Researchers are now testing cooling interventions, including tree planting, reflective surfaces, and community-designed cooling centers. The project aims to inform citywide climate resilience planning and public health outreach.

content_items.title: Campus Energy Upgrades Cut Emissions and Costs in 2021
content_items.summary: A series of energy-efficiency projects across campus buildings reduces emissions and advances sustainability goals.
content_items.body:
Facilities staff and sustainability researchers partnered to retrofit lighting, upgrade building controls, and optimize heating and cooling systems across 12 academic buildings. Early results show a 14 percent reduction in energy use intensity and significant utility cost savings.

The project serves as a living lab, giving students a chance to analyze real-time building performance data. Lessons from these upgrades are guiding the university’s long-term climate action plan and demonstrating how campus operations can align with research-driven sustainability strategies.

content_items.title: Climate-Smart Transportation Plan Reimagines Campus Commuting
content_items.summary: New data-driven transportation initiatives reduce campus greenhouse gas emissions while improving mobility options.
content_items.body:
Sustainability researchers partnered with campus planners to model how changes to shuttle routes, bike infrastructure, and parking policy could cut transportation-related emissions. The resulting climate-smart transportation plan informed the launch of protected bike lanes, an expanded electric shuttle fleet, and a commuter incentive program.

Modeling suggests the plan could reduce commuting emissions by up to 30 percent over five years. Ongoing monitoring will evaluate how behavior changes over time and which strategies are most effective in supporting climate change goals.

content_items.title: Campus Reaches New Zero-Waste and Recycling Milestones
content_items.summary: Expanded composting, recycling education, and waste audits move campus operations closer to a zero-waste goal.
content_items.body:
In 2023, the university expanded composting collection to all residence halls, launched new sorting stations in dining facilities, and ran student-led waste audits at major events. As a result, the campus diverted 62 percent of waste from landfills—its highest rate to date.

Sustainability staff and students are using the data to refine outreach campaigns and identify remaining barriers to waste reduction. The zero-waste initiative is a key component of the institution’s broader sustainability framework and offers experiential learning opportunities in environmental management.

content_items.title: COVID-19: Initial Campus Response and Transition to Remote Learning
content_items.summary: The university announces its first comprehensive response to COVID-19, including a transition to remote instruction and enhanced health measures.
content_items.body:
In response to emerging public health guidance on COVID-19, university leadership announced a temporary suspension of in-person classes and events beginning March 16, 2020. Instruction moved online for the remainder of the semester, with faculty receiving additional support for remote teaching.

The announcement outlined enhanced cleaning protocols, new hand-sanitizer stations across campus, and guidance for students returning home or remaining in residence halls. Officials emphasized that plans would be revisited as new information became available from public health authorities.

content_items.title: COVID-19: Fall 2021 Health and Safety Guidance
content_items.summary: Updated COVID-19 protocols for the fall 2021 semester, including vaccination expectations, masking, and testing.
content_items.body:
As students prepare to return for the fall 2021 semester, the university has released updated COVID-19 guidance. The policy strongly encourages vaccination, requires masks in classrooms and public indoor spaces, and continues surveillance testing for unvaccinated community members.

The announcement also details procedures for reporting positive cases, obtaining accommodations, and accessing mental health resources. Campus officials stress that protocols may be adjusted in response to changing public health conditions.

content_items.title: Men’s Basketball Rolls to Home-Opener Win Over River Ridge
content_items.summary: The Lions start their 2023–24 season with a statement win, powered by a dominant second half in front of a packed home crowd.
content_items.body:
The men’s basketball team opened its 2023–24 campaign with an 82–68 home victory over River Ridge. After trailing by four at halftime, the Lions used a 15–2 run early in the second half to seize control. Junior guard Marcus Lee led all scorers with 24 points, while first-year forward Daniel Ortiz added a double-double off the bench.

Head coach Carla Nguyen praised the energy of the student section and highlighted the team’s balanced scoring and defensive adjustments. The win sets an early tone for a season in which the Lions aim to contend for a conference title.
content_items.notable_highlights: Marcus Lee scored 24 points and the Lions used a 15–2 second-half run to pull away.

content_items.title: Women’s Soccer Battles to Draw in Home Match Against Hillcrest
content_items.summary: A late equalizer preserves a home draw as the Lions create chances under the lights against Hillcrest.
content_items.body:
In a tightly contested non-conference match, the women’s soccer team earned a 1–1 draw against Hillcrest on their home field. After conceding a goal in the 35th minute, the Lions pressed high in the second half, finally breaking through when senior forward Amina Cole scored off a corner kick in the 78th minute.

Head coach Roberto Alvarez highlighted the team’s resilience and defensive organization, noting that the match provided valuable preparation for upcoming conference play. The result moves the Lions to 4–1–2 on the season.
content_items.notable_highlights: Senior forward Amina Cole scored the equalizer on a late corner kick.

content_items.title: First-Generation Scholar Finds Community and Confidence on Campus
content_items.summary: Supported by a need-based scholarship, first-generation student Maya Lopez reflects on her path to college and the communities she has found on campus.
content_items.body:
When biology major Maya Lopez arrived on campus as a first-generation college student, she worried about fitting in and managing costs. A need-based scholarship eased the financial burden and allowed her to focus on academics and involvement in student organizations.

Maya now mentors other first-generation students, serves on the scholarship advisory council, and plans to pursue medical school. She credits her scholarship with giving her the confidence to take on leadership roles and imagine new possibilities for her future.

content_items.title: Undergraduate Research Scholarship Keeps Lab Dreams Alive During Pandemic
content_items.summary: Chemistry major Jordan Patel uses an undergraduate research scholarship to continue lab work and present virtual findings during the pandemic.
content_items.body:
As campus labs shifted to new safety protocols in 2020, chemistry major Jordan Patel worried that research opportunities might disappear. An undergraduate research scholarship allowed Jordan to remain engaged in a faculty-led project, splitting time between carefully scheduled lab shifts and data analysis at home.

Jordan later presented their findings at the university’s virtual research symposium, gaining experience in communicating science to a broad audience. The scholarship, Jordan says, made it possible to stay on track for graduate school while deepening their love for discovery.

content_items.title: Global Scholars Scholarship Supports Virtual International Exchange
content_items.summary: With travel restricted, Global Scholars use scholarship funding to engage in virtual international coursework and collaborative projects.
content_items.body:
When study abroad programs paused, recipients of the Global Scholars scholarship reimagined what international learning could look like. Through virtual exchange courses, joint research projects, and online cultural events, students connected with peers at partner universities around the world.

Political science major Sara Kim says the scholarship gave her the flexibility to enroll in additional language courses and participate in a capstone project on global development. The experience affirmed her interest in international law and policy.

content_items.title: Leadership Scholarship Helps Student Turn Campus Ideas Into Action
content_items.summary: A campus leadership scholarship enables business major Andre Williams to launch new initiatives that support peer success.
content_items.body:
Andre Williams arrived on campus with big ideas about mentoring and community-building but wasn’t sure how to balance work, finances, and involvement. A leadership scholarship reduced the need for off-campus employment, freeing time to serve as a residence hall advisor and student organization president.

Andre helped launch a peer coaching network that connects first-year students with upper-division mentors. He plans to pursue a career in student affairs and credits the scholarship with making it possible to explore leadership paths.

content_items.title: Microrobots Navigate the Bloodstream in Engineering Research Highlight
content_items.summary: A short research highlight video showcases engineering students developing microrobots that could one day deliver targeted therapies.
content_items.body:
In this under-five-minute research highlight, faculty and students in the Microrobotics Lab explain how they design and test tiny devices capable of navigating fluid environments. The video offers a behind-the-scenes look at prototype fabrication, imaging techniques, and simulations used to predict microrobot behavior.

The team envisions medical applications such as targeted drug delivery and minimally invasive diagnostics. They also discuss ethical considerations and interdisciplinary collaborations with clinicians.

content_items.title: Alumni-Led Fintech Startup Scales Up With Campus Partnerships
content_items.summary: Graduates of the business and computer science programs grow their fintech startup while mentoring current students.
content_items.body:
Three alumni who met in a campus entrepreneurship course have grown their fintech startup from a capstone project into a venture-backed company. The team’s platform helps students and families better understand financial aid packages and long-term repayment scenarios.

Through an ongoing partnership with the university’s innovation hub, the founders host workshops, offer internships, and mentor student teams. They say the experience of launching a startup as undergraduates prepared them for the challenges of scaling in a fast-moving industry.

content_items.title: Alumni GreenTech Startup Pilots Solar Monitoring on Campus
content_items.summary: Engineering alumni return to campus to test smart solar monitoring technology developed by their sustainability-focused startup.
content_items.body:
A team of engineering alumni founded a GreenTech startup that builds smart monitoring systems for solar arrays. This spring, they partnered with the university to pilot their technology on rooftop installations at two campus buildings.

The collaboration gives current students a chance to analyze real-time performance data and learn about the startup lifecycle. The founders hope the pilot will lead to broader adoption of their system at universities and municipalities across the region.

content_items.title: Orientation Week 2024: Campus Life in Full Color
content_items.summary: From the welcome ceremony to late-night events, Orientation Week 2024 brings new students into campus life.
content_items.body:
Orientation Week 2024 welcomed one of the university’s most diverse incoming classes with a full slate of events. New students gathered on the main quad for the formal welcome ceremony, met orientation leaders during small-group sessions, and explored academic and support resources at the resource fair.

Evening programs ranged from a student organization showcase to a comedy night and outdoor movie. Staff and student volunteers emphasized building connections, highlighting wellness resources, and introducing campus traditions that define the undergraduate experience.

content_items.title: Orientation Week 2024: Move-In Day Moments
content_items.summary: Families, friends, and volunteers help new students settle into residence halls during Move-In Day 2024.
content_items.body:
Move-In Day 2024 brought full parking lots, rolling carts, and plenty of emotions as new students arrived at residence halls. Orientation leaders, housing staff, and volunteers helped unload cars and direct families to check-in stations.

Many students personalized their rooms with posters, photos, and mementos from home, while residence hall staff hosted floor meetings to introduce key resources. For many new Lions, the day marked the start of a new chapter and lasting friendships.

content_items.title: Orientation Week 2024: Academic Success and Support Highlights
content_items.summary: Workshops and advising sessions introduce new students to the academic resources that will support their success.
content_items.body:
Throughout Orientation Week 2024, new students attended sessions focused on academic expectations, time management, and available support services. Faculty panels described what learning looks like in first-year courses, while advisors met with students to discuss course schedules and long-term plans.

The Academic Success Center hosted open houses showcasing tutoring, writing support, and study-skills coaching. Students also learned how to access library resources, technology help, and research opportunities early in their academic careers.

content_items.title: AI Research Labs Launch Cross-Campus Collaboration on Responsible AI
content_items.summary: Computer science, engineering, and humanities scholars team up to study artificial intelligence, ethics, and real-world impact.
content_items.body:
The university’s AI Research Labs have launched a new collaboration bringing together experts in computer science, engineering, philosophy, and law. The initiative focuses on designing artificial intelligence systems that are transparent, fair, and aligned with societal values.

Teams will investigate topics ranging from algorithmic bias in healthcare to human–robot interaction in public spaces. The collaboration also expands opportunities for students to engage in AI research, including new seminar courses and undergraduate research assistant positions.
